content.text: What Is the Cloud Solution Provider (CSP) Program?

The Cloud Solution Provider (CSP) program lets Microsoft partners resell Azure subscriptions to customers. Instead of the customer paying Microsoft directly, the customer pays the CSP partner, and the partner pays Microsoft. The partner takes on billing, first-line support, and often managed services on top of the Azure platform itself.

From a technical standpoint, Azure resources in a CSP subscription run in the same Microsoft data centres and on the same platform as any other Azure subscription. The difference is entirely in billing and support flow: Microsoft invoices the partner, and the partner invoices the customer.

attrs.alt: CSP billing models: Direct (partner buys from Microsoft) and Indirect (provider → reseller → customer)

content.text: The CSP program has two tiers.
Reseller provides Tier 1; Provider Tier 2; Microsoft Tier 3

Direct CSP partners must meet Microsoft's partner competency requirements (Gold/Solutions Partner designation) and maintain a 24/7 support capability. Most smaller MSPs operate as Indirect Resellers through a distributor.
How CSP Subscriptions Differ From Direct Azure
A CSP subscription looks nearly identical to a regular Azure subscription from inside the Azure portal, but there are important differences in billing, support, and lifecycle.
Partner Admin Link or Partner Center
Only on Free Trial / VS subscriptions
Partner Center: The CSP Management Hub

The CSP partner manages customer subscriptions primarily through Partner Center (partner.microsoft.com), not the Azure portal. Partner Center is where partners create customers, provision subscriptions, manage billing, and view commission and incentives.
Customer management: create and manage customer tenants, provision subscriptions.
Billing: view monthly invoices from Microsoft, generate customer invoices, manage credit limits.
Support: submit service requests on behalf of customers, track status.
Licenses: manage Microsoft 365, Dynamics 365, and Azure subscriptions in one place.
Incentives: track partner-earned credits and co-op marketing funds.

Partners link their partner ID (MPN ID) to the Azure credentials they use to manage customer environments via Partner Admin Link. This allows Microsoft to attribute revenue to the correct partner for incentive and recognition programs — it does not grant any additional access.

# Link your partner ID to the credentials you use to manage customer environments
# Run this once per credential (user or service principal) that manages customer Azure resources

# Install the extension if needed
az extension add --name managementpartner

# Check if a PAL link already exists
az managementpartner show

# Create the PAL link with your Microsoft Partner Network (MPN) ID
az managementpartner create --partner-id "<your-mpn-id>"

# Output confirms: {"partnerId": "1234567", "partnerName": "Contoso MSP", ...}

# Update the link (e.g., after org changes)
az managementpartner update --partner-id "<new-mpn-id>"

# Delete the link
az managementpartner delete --partner-id "<mpn-id>"

When a CSP partner provisions a subscription for a customer, they need access to manage it. There are two mechanisms: the legacy Delegated Admin Privileges (DAP) and the newer Granular Delegated Admin Privileges (GDAP).
Global Admin + Helpdesk Admin in customer tenant
Specific Azure AD roles (principle of least privilege)

Microsoft began requiring CSP partners to migrate from DAP to GDAP in 2023 as a security hardening measure. DAP gives the partner Global Administrator access to the customer tenant — GDAP scopes this to specific roles and time windows.

# GDAP relationships are managed in Partner Center (portal, not CLI)
# From the customer side, you can view what GDAP access exists

# List service principals in your tenant that belong to a CSP partner
az ad sp list \
  --filter "appDisplayName eq 'Partner Customer Delegated Admin Offline Processor'" \
  --query "[].{Id:id,DisplayName:displayName,AppId:appId}" -o table

# View role assignments granted to CSP partner security groups
az role assignment list --all \
  --query "[?contains(principalName,'foreignGroupObjectId')].{Principal:principalName,Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

# As a customer, review and audit CSP partner access to your subscription
az role assignment list \
  --subscription "<customer-sub-id>" \
  --all \
  --query "[?principalType=='ForeignGroup' || contains(principalName,'partner')].{Role:roleDefinitionName,Principal:principalName,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table
Azure Resources in a CSP Subscription

Deploying and managing Azure resources in a CSP subscription uses the same Azure CLI, portal, and API experience as any other subscription. The customer (or the partner acting on behalf of the customer) can use all the same ARM templates, Bicep files, and automation tools.

# Verify you are working in the correct CSP subscription
az account show --query "{Name:name,Id:id,TenantId:tenantId,State:state}" -o table

# Check the offer type to confirm CSP
az account show --query "subscriptionPolicies.quotaId" -o tsv
# CSP subscriptions return: MSDN_2014-09-01 or AzureInCsp_2015-05-01

# Create resources exactly as you would in any subscription
az group create --name "rg-webapp-prod" --location eastus
az webapp create \
  --name "customer-webapp-prod" \
  --resource-group "rg-webapp-prod" \
  --plan "customer-asp" \
  --runtime "NODE:18-lts"

# Check what resource providers are registered in the CSP subscription
az provider list --query "[?registrationState=='Registered'].namespace" -o tsv | head -20

# Some resource providers require explicit registration
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.Compute
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.Network
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.Storage
Monitoring Costs in a CSP Subscription

Cost Management is available in CSP subscriptions but with some differences. The customer can see cost data in the Azure portal; the partner sees aggregated billing data in Partner Center. Setting up budgets and alerts in the customer subscription works the same as in direct subscriptions.

# Create a budget in a CSP customer subscription
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "monthly-budget" \
  --amount 1000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date "2026-07-01" \
  --end-date "2027-06-30" \
  --notifications '{
    "actual_80": {
      "enabled": true,
      "operator": "GreaterThan",
      "threshold": 80,
      "contactEmails": ["customer-ops@example.com","partner-am@csp.com"],
      "thresholdType": "Actual"
    }
  }'

# Query current month costs in the CSP subscription
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<csp-sub-id>" \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity None \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"ServiceName"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table

In CSP, the partner is responsible for first-line and second-line support. The customer contacts the partner (not Microsoft) for support requests. The partner escalates to Microsoft only for platform-level issues that the partner cannot resolve — this is called Escalation to Microsoft (ETM) or Partner Support.
Account questions, basic troubleshooting, subscription management, billing queries
Complex configuration issues, architecture guidance, deeper Azure debugging
Azure platform bugs, data centre incidents, undocumented API behaviour

# Partners submit support requests on behalf of customers via the Azure portal
# using the customer's subscription context (after GDAP access)

# Set context to the customer subscription
az account set --subscription "<customer-sub-id>"

# View open support tickets in the customer subscription
az support tickets list \
  --query "[?status=='open'].{TicketId:name,Title:title,Severity:severity,CreatedDate:createdDate}" \
  -o table

# Create a support ticket on behalf of the customer (as CSP partner with delegated access)
az support tickets create \
  --ticket-name "csp-customer-issue-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M)" \
  --title "VM connectivity issue after NSG rule change" \
  --description "Customer reports VM in rg-prod is unreachable on port 443 after NSG update at 14:00 UTC. All other VMs in same VNet are accessible." \
  --problem-classification /providers/Microsoft.Support/services/virtual-machines-service-guid/problemClassifications/connectivity \
  --severity Sev2 \
  --contact-first-name "Partner" \
  --contact-last-name "Support" \
  --contact-email support@csp-partner.com \
  --contact-country US \
  --contact-preferred-time-zone "UTC" \
  --contact-preferred-support-language en-US
Transferring a Subscription Out of CSP

If a customer decides to move from CSP billing to a direct EA or MCA billing relationship with Microsoft, the CSP subscription must be transferred. This is a multi-step process that requires coordination between the customer, the partner, and Microsoft.
Customer or partner initiates a transfer request in the Azure portal (Subscriptions → Transfer billing ownership).
The recipient (new billing account owner — EA or MCA) accepts the transfer.
Billing shifts to the new billing account; Azure resources continue running without interruption.
The CSP partner loses billing relationship but may retain RBAC access if the customer keeps them.
Any active reservations purchased by the partner must be cancelled or transferred separately.

# Verify subscription details before initiating transfer
az account show --subscription "<csp-sub-id>" \
  --query "{Name:name,Id:id,Tenant:tenantId,Offer:subscriptionPolicies.quotaId}" -o table

# After transfer, confirm the new billing relationship
az account show --subscription "<csp-sub-id>" \
  --query "subscriptionPolicies.quotaId" -o tsv
# Should now return the EA or MCA offer type instead of AzureInCsp_2015-05-01

# Verify RBAC assignments post-transfer (CSP partner's groups may still have access)
az role assignment list \
  --subscription "<sub-id>" \
  --all \
  --query "[?contains(principalName,'ForeignGroup') || principalType=='ForeignGroup'].{Role:roleDefinitionName,Principal:principalName}" \
  -o table
Automating CSP Customer Management with the Partner Center API

Partners managing dozens or hundreds of customers typically automate customer provisioning, subscription management, and billing reporting through the Partner Center REST API. Microsoft provides official SDK libraries for .NET, Java, and PowerShell, but the REST API is also accessible via raw HTTP calls or the Azure CLI with appropriate credentials.
Authenticating with the Partner Center API

# The Partner Center API uses OAuth 2.0 with the partner tenant credentials
# You need an Azure AD app registration in the PARTNER tenant with Partner Center API permissions

# Get a token for the Partner Center API
PARTNER_TENANT="<partner-tenant-id>"
APP_ID="<partner-center-app-id>"
APP_SECRET="<partner-center-app-secret>"

TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST \
  "https://login.microsoftonline.com/${PARTNER_TENANT}/oauth2/token" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  -d "client_id=${APP_ID}" \
  -d "client_secret=${APP_SECRET}" \
  -d "resource=https://api.partnercenter.microsoft.com" \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['access_token'])")

echo "Token acquired (length: ${#TOKEN})"

# List all CSP customers
curl -s "https://api.partnercenter.microsoft.com/v1/customers" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "MS-CorrelationId: $(python3 -c 'import uuid; print(uuid.uuid4())')" \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"id"|"companyName"' | head -20

# List all Azure subscriptions for a specific customer
CUSTOMER_ID="<customer-tenant-id>"
TOKEN="<partner-center-token>"

curl -s "https://api.partnercenter.microsoft.com/v1/customers/${CUSTOMER_ID}/subscriptions" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
for sub in data.get('items', []):
    print(f"  {sub['id']:40} {sub['friendlyName']:30} {sub['status']}  {sub['offerName']}")"

# Suspend a customer's subscription (e.g. non-payment)
CUSTOMER_ID="<customer-tenant-id>"
SUB_ID="<csp-subscription-id>"
TOKEN="<partner-center-token>"

curl -s -X PATCH \
  "https://api.partnercenter.microsoft.com/v1/customers/${CUSTOMER_ID}/subscriptions/${SUB_ID}" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"status": "suspended", "autoRenewEnabled": false}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool

# Reinstate the subscription after payment is received
curl -s -X PATCH \
  "https://api.partnercenter.microsoft.com/v1/customers/${CUSTOMER_ID}/subscriptions/${SUB_ID}" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"status": "active", "autoRenewEnabled": true}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool

# Note: suspended CSP subscriptions enter a 30-day grace period before resources are deleted
# During grace period resources are stopped but not yet deleted
# After 30 days, all resources are permanently deleted

CSP partners often combine CSP billing with Azure Lighthouse for resource management. CSP provides the billing relationship and subscription ownership; Lighthouse provides fine-grained, auditable access to the customer's Azure resources without requiring guest accounts in the customer tenant.

CSP GDAP gives the partner Azure AD-level access (Microsoft Entra roles). Azure Lighthouse gives the partner ARM-level access (Azure RBAC roles). They are complementary: GDAP for identity/AAD operations; Lighthouse for resource management within a subscription.

# Scenario: partner manages customer resources via Lighthouse, not GDAP
# Step 1: As CSP partner, deploy Lighthouse delegation into the customer subscription
# (using GDAP access to run the deployment in the customer subscription)

az account set --subscription "<customer-sub-id>"

az deployment sub create \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file lighthouse-delegation.json \
  --parameters '{
    "mspOfferName":      {"value": "CSP Partner Azure Management"},
    "managedByTenantId": {"value": "<partner-tenant-id>"},
    "authorizations": {"value": [
      {
        "principalId": "<partner-engineers-group-id>",
        "roleDefinitionId": "b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c",
        "principalIdDisplayName": "CSP Engineering Team"
      },
      {
        "principalId": "<partner-monitoring-group-id>",
        "roleDefinitionId": "acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7",
        "principalIdDisplayName": "CSP Monitoring Team"
      }
    ]}
  }'

# Step 2: From partner tenant, access customer subscription (no GDAP needed now)
az managedservices assignment list -o table
az account set --subscription "<customer-sub-id>"
az resource list --subscription "<customer-sub-id>" -o table
Multi-Customer Automation: The CSP Partner Scale Pattern

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# csp-fleet-report.sh — gather VM count and cost summary across all CSP customers
# Uses Partner Center API + ARM API with GDAP or Lighthouse access

PARTNER_TENANT="<partner-tenant-id>"
APP_ID="<app-id>"
APP_SECRET="<app-secret>"

# 1. Get Partner Center token
PC_TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST \
  "https://login.microsoftonline.com/${PARTNER_TENANT}/oauth2/token" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=${APP_ID}&client_secret=${APP_SECRET}" \
  -d "resource=https://api.partnercenter.microsoft.com" \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['access_token'])")

# 2. Get all customers
CUSTOMERS=$(curl -s "https://api.partnercenter.microsoft.com/v1/customers" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $PC_TOKEN" \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
for c in data.get('items', []):
    print(c['id'], c['companyProfile']['companyName'])
")

# 3. For each customer, get their subscriptions and resource counts
while IFS=' ' read -r CUSTOMER_ID CUSTOMER_NAME; do
  echo "=== $CUSTOMER_NAME ($CUSTOMER_ID) ==="

  # Get ARM token for the customer tenant
  ARM_TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token \
    --tenant "$CUSTOMER_ID" \
    --resource https://management.azure.com \
    --query accessToken -o tsv 2>/dev/null || echo "ACCESS_DENIED")

  if [ "$ARM_TOKEN" != "ACCESS_DENIED" ]; then
    VM_COUNT=$(curl -s "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions?api-version=2020-01-01" \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer $ARM_TOKEN" \
      | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(len(json.load(sys.stdin).get('value',[])))")
    echo "  Subscriptions: $VM_COUNT"
  else
    echo "  No delegated access configured"
  fi
done <<< "$CUSTOMERS"

When a CSP partner actively manages a customer's Azure environment — evidenced by holding an eligible RBAC role on the subscription or resource group — Microsoft applies a Partner-Earned Credit (PEC) discount to the partner's cost. PEC reduces what the partner pays Microsoft for that customer's Azure consumption by up to 15%.
PEC is earned per-resource: the discount applies to individual resources where the partner holds Owner, Contributor, or specific built-in roles.
PEC is calculated automatically — no manual claiming is required.
If the partner loses RBAC access to a resource, PEC stops immediately for that resource.
Indirect Resellers can earn PEC on resources they actively manage; Indirect Providers earn PEC on resources the reseller is not managing.

# Verify PEC eligibility: confirm the partner has RBAC access on customer resources
# (PEC requires Owner, Contributor, or an eligible built-in role)

# Check RBAC assignments in the customer subscription
az role assignment list \
  --subscription "<customer-sub-id>" \
  --all \
  --query "[?principalType=='ServicePrincipal' || principalType=='ForeignGroup'].{Role:roleDefinitionName,Principal:principalName,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

# PEC-eligible roles include:
# Owner, Contributor, Network Contributor, Storage Account Contributor,
# Virtual Machine Contributor, SQL DB Contributor, and others.
# Check the Partner Center documentation for the full list.

# View PEC amounts in Partner Center billing (REST API)
BILLING_PERIOD="2026-06"
curl -s "https://api.partnercenter.microsoft.com/v1/invoices/unbilled/lineitems?provider=Azure&invoiceLineItemType=UsageLineItems&currencyCode=USD&period=current" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $PC_TOKEN" \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
for item in data.get('items', [])[:5]:
    print(f"  {item.get('subscriptionDescription','')[:30]:30}  PEC: {item.get('partnerEarnedCreditPercentage',0)}%")
"
Partner loses GDAP access after relationship expires
Cannot access customer subscription to manage resources
Set GDAP relationships to auto-renew; monitor expiry dates in Partner Center
Customer creates direct support ticket with Microsoft
Microsoft routes to partner; customer frustrated by redirect
Communicate support path clearly at onboarding; configure support plan via CSP
All resources stopped; data at risk after 30-day grace period
Implement automated billing alerts; have escalation path for at-risk accounts
Partner-Earned Credit lost due to missing RBAC
Partner pays full price for resources they manage
Audit RBAC assignments regularly; ensure partner service principal has eligible role
Reserved Instance not transferred; partner still paying
Coordinate RI cancellation or transfer before subscription transfer

The Cloud Solution Provider program lets Microsoft partners provision, bill, and support Azure subscriptions on behalf of customers. The partner pays Microsoft, bills the customer, and provides first-line support. The two models are Direct (partner buys from Microsoft) and Indirect (distributor/provider → reseller → customer). Partners use Partner Center for customer and billing management and must link their MPN partner ID to customer credentials via Partner Admin Link to receive incentives. Access to customer environments is provided through GDAP (the modern, least-privilege replacement for legacy Delegated Admin Privileges). From a resource perspective, CSP subscriptions behave identically to direct subscriptions — the same Azure CLI, APIs, and deployment tools apply.
